policy_statement: Achieve zero-emission transit fleets across Chicagoland by 2040.
mechanism_description: Electrifying transit fleets shifts vehicles from fossil fuel combustion to zero-emission propulsion, reducing transportation-sector greenhouse gas emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: ICE fleet operation → vehicle electrification → ↓ fossil fuel combustion per mile (tailpipe eliminated) → ↓ lifecycle transport GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change: Fleet operators (city fleets, transit, taxis/ride-hail, delivery, freight) replace ICE vehicles with battery-electric (or other zero-emission) vehicles and add/enable charging operations.  
(2) Process: Eliminating on-board fuel combustion removes tailpipe CO2 and reduces upstream emissions depending on grid carbon intensity; EV drivetrains are more energy-efficient, further lowering energy demand per mile.  
(3) Outcome: Net reduction in transportation-sector CO2e emissions (often immediate tailpipe reductions and progressively lower lifecycle emissions as electricity generation decarbonizes).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced GHG emissions from transportation by substituting electric drivetrains for fossil-fuel combustion in fleet vehicles.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: switching fleets from ICE to electric reduces fossil-fuel combustion and thus lowers GHG emissions—this is a direct mitigation pathway.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: while electrification can improve local air quality or reduce noise, the mechanism as stated and used in climate plans is principally to cut CO2e from transport; it does not primarily manage climate hazards (adaptation), nor is it chiefly about using fewer resources for the same service (resource efficiency as a primary frame), nor does it rely on ecosystems (nature-based solutions).  
3) Secondary categories: none are inherent across all instances—air quality benefits or energy-efficiency gains are common but not guaranteed to be climate-relevant as a separate category, and they are not the primary classification schema here.  
4) Edge cases applied: if the electricity supply is very carbon-intensive, near-term lifecycle GHG reductions may be smaller, but the intended and typical dominant effect of fleet electrification policies remains transport decarbonization.  
5) Label applies to ALL instances: any policy implementing fleet electrification shares the same dominant mitigation mechanism—replacing combustion with electricity to reduce transport emissions.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Lifecycle emissions benefits depend on grid mix and charging behavior; in highly fossil-based grids or where EV adoption induces additional vehicle miles traveled, net GHG reductions may be reduced, but the dominant intended mechanism remains mitigation via transport emissions reduction.
co_benefits: Improved local air quality (NOx/PM), reduced noise, potential public health benefits, potential operating cost savings.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be implemented via regulatory mandates, procurement requirements, or incentives, but the provided text only states an end-state goal and date, so it is classified as target-setting.
